question: Build a debounced type-ahead search box.
answer: Bind an input to a FormControl, listen to valueChanges, then debounceTime + distinctUntilChanged + switchMap to the API so stale requests are cancelled. Render results with the async pipe.
tip: switchMap is the key — it cancels the in-flight request when the user keeps typing.

question: Build a client-side pagination component.
answer: Keep page and pageSize in state; derive the current slice with a getter (or computed signal). Render page buttons from Math.ceil(total / pageSize) and guard prev/next at the bounds.
tip: Derive the visible slice — never mutate the source array when paginating.

question: Implement infinite scroll.
answer: Place a sentinel element at the list's end and observe it with IntersectionObserver (or the CDK scrolling module). When it enters the viewport, load and append the next page. Clean up the observer in ngOnDestroy.
tip: IntersectionObserver beats a scroll-event listener — no throttling needed, less jank.

question: Build a reusable star-rating component.
answer: Render N stars from an array; fill up to value, track a hover index for preview, and emit the chosen value. For form integration, implement ControlValueAccessor so it works with [(ngModel)]/reactive forms.
tip: Implementing ControlValueAccessor turns any widget into a first-class form control.

question: Build a reusable modal/dialog component.
answer: Use *ngIf (or the native <dialog>) with ng-content to project arbitrary content. Emit a close event, close on backdrop click and Esc (@HostListener), and trap focus for accessibility.
tip: Bonus points for focus trapping and Esc-to-close — that's the accessibility part interviewers watch for.

question: Build a todo list (add / toggle / delete / filter).
answer: Hold an array of { id, text, done }. Add with an incrementing id, toggle by mapping immutably, delete by filtering, and derive the filtered view (all/active/done) with a getter. Keep updates immutable for OnPush.
tip: Update immutably (map/filter returning new arrays) so it plays well with OnPush.

question: Build a reusable sortable data table.
answer: Accept columns and rows as inputs; use ng-template with ngTemplateOutlet for custom cell rendering. Sort by a clicked column, toggling asc/desc, on an immutable copy. Add trackBy for performance.
tip: Generic inputs + a template for cells is what makes a table truly reusable.

question: Build a tabs component with content projection.
answer: Create a TabComponent (with a title input and a projected body) and a TabsComponent that collects them via @ContentChildren. Render the tab headers, track the active one, and show only its content.
tip: @ContentChildren(TabComponent) is the pattern that powers Material tabs.

question: Build a countdown timer.
answer: Use RxJS timer(0, 1000) to tick each second, map to the remaining time, takeWhile until zero, and render via the async pipe (auto-unsubscribes). Avoids manual setInterval cleanup.
tip: The async pipe + takeWhile means zero manual subscription management.

question: Build a form with a dynamic list of fields.
answer: Use a FormArray of controls/groups. Provide add() (push a new control) and remove(i) (removeAt), and iterate controls in the template with the array's formArrayName.
tip: FormArray is the canonical answer to 'add/remove fields at runtime'.

question: Show a different block based on a radio-button selection.
answer: Bind the radios to a single property with [(ngModel)], then use @if / *ngIf (or @switch) to render the matching block.
tip: One model property drives both the radios and the conditional blocks.
code:
Option A Option B
Div A
Div B

question: Transform a list of employee objects to only { id, name } using RxJS.
answer: Use the RxJS map operator on the stream, and Array.map inside it to reshape each object.
tip: Two maps: the RxJS map transforms the stream, the array map transforms each item.

question: Chain two API calls where the second depends on the first.
answer: Use switchMap so the second call runs with the first response, and any in-flight first call is cancelled if a new one starts.
tip: switchMap for dependent calls; concatMap if order matters and none should cancel.

question: Run several API calls in parallel and act once all complete.
answer: Use forkJoin to fire independent calls concurrently; it emits once, with all results, after every call completes (like Promise.all).
tip: forkJoin waits for all to COMPLETE — great for parallel GETs before rendering a page.

question: Share one API result across 3 components without repeating the call.
answer: Put the data in a shared service: fetch once, cache it in a BehaviorSubject (or shareReplay(1)), and let all components subscribe to the same stream. The HTTP call runs a single time.
tip: BehaviorSubject holds the latest value so late-subscribing components get it instantly.

question: Build a custom Autocomplete component (without Angular Material).
answer: Use a FormControl and subscribe to valueChanges. Apply debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged, and switchMap to fetch suggestions. Show a dropdown below the input and hide it when an option is selected or focus is lost.
tip: Interviewers mainly look for debounce, switchMap and dropdown handling.

question: Implement Drag & Drop using Angular CDK.
answer: Use Angular CDK's cdkDropList and cdkDrag. Handle the cdkDropListDropped event and reorder items using moveItemInArray().
tip: Angular CDK Drag & Drop is asked much more frequently than writing drag logic manually.

question: Build a Nested Comments / Tree View component.
answer: Create a recursive component that accepts a node with children. Display the current node and recursively render its child nodes using the same component.
tip: Recursive components are the standard solution for tree structures.

question: Build a Kanban Board with draggable cards.
answer: Create multiple cdkDropList containers (Todo, In Progress, Done). Enable moving cards between lists using transferArrayItem().
tip: This combines Drag & Drop, component communication and state management.

question: Build a Light / Dark Theme Switcher.
answer: Maintain the selected theme in a service. Toggle a CSS class on the document body and persist the user's preference using localStorage.
tip: Remember to restore the saved theme during application startup.

question: Build a Login Page with username and password validation.
answer: Create a Reactive Form with username and password controls. Apply required, minimum length and email validators where appropriate. Show validation messages only after the field is touched or the form is submitted.
tip: Most Angular interviews expect Reactive Forms instead of Template Driven Forms.
